review.summary: The series is close, but it still has correctness gaps in boot/image loading and in how endianness is derived for implicit/translated memory accesses. The most serious issue is that page-table walks are switched on current privilege rather than supervisor translation endianness, which breaks configurations where UBE and SBE differ. There is also a clear boot regression risk because ELF loading remains hardcoded to little-endian, and the new per-CPU property is consumed as if endianness were uniform across the hart array.

A.summary: Reviewed the cover letter and visible patches 1/7 through 5/7, focusing on CPU state, translation state, boot ROM generation, and MMU/page-table access paths.

B.summary: Cross-checked the patch intent against RISC-V endianness rules: instructions stay little-endian, explicit data accesses depend on effective privilege, and implicit page-table accesses follow supervisor translation endianness rather than user endianness.

C.summary: Identified 3 actionable correctness issues: hardcoded ELF endianness in boot loading, wrong endianness source for page-table walks, and a mismatch between per-hart configurability and hart-array-wide boot data generation.

D.summary: Risk is moderate to high for big-endian boot and MMU bring-up. The page-table issue can cause faults/hangs once MMU is enabled, and the ELF-loading issue can reject or misparse BE kernel images.

E.summary: Recommendation is to respin after fixing the endianness source for MMU/page-table operations, making ELF loading actually select ELFDATA2MSB for BE kernels, and either enforcing uniform hart-array endianness or handling mixed-endian hart arrays explicitly.

findings.title: Page-table walk endianness is taken from current privilege instead of supervisor translation state
findings.description: The page walker now chooses `address_space_ldl_be/ldq_be` based on `riscv_cpu_data_is_big_endian(env)`, but that helper keys off `env->priv` and therefore returns UBE while executing in U-mode. For address translation, implicit accesses to page tables are supervisor memory-management accesses and must follow supervisor translation endianness, not user data endianness. If `UBE != SBE`, user accesses will walk page tables with the wrong byte order and can fault or hang once the MMU is active.
findings.suggestion: Derive page-table-walk endianness from the translation context (`mmu_idx` / effective translation mode), not from `env->priv`. In practice this should use supervisor translation endianness for S/U page tables, and the appropriate virtualization-specific endianness where applicable, independent of UBE.
findings.confidence_reason: This follows directly from the privileged-spec rule for implicit accesses to supervisor memory-management structures and from the helper implementation shown in patch 3, which only switches on `env->priv`.

findings.title: Kernel ELF loading is still hardcoded to little-endian
findings.description: The commit message says boot code should pass `ELFDATA2MSB` or `ELFDATA2LSB` based on runtime endianness, but the actual `load_elf_ram_sym()` call still passes `ELFDATA2LSB` unconditionally. A big-endian RISC-V ELF kernel image will therefore still be parsed as little-endian at load time, which defeats the advertised BE boot support.
patch_context: kernel_size = load_elf_ram_sym(kernel_filename, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL,
findings.suggestion: Actually select the ELF class endianness argument from `riscv_is_big_endian(harts)`, using `ELFDATA2MSB` for BE and `ELFDATA2LSB` for LE.
findings.confidence_reason: The bug is explicit in the posted diff: only formatting changed and the endianness argument remained hardcoded to `ELFDATA2LSB`.

findings.title: Boot code treats endianness as hart-array-wide even though the new property is per-CPU
findings.description: Patch 1 introduces `big-endian` as a per-CPU property and the cover letter says machine models can configure individual HARTs. Patch 4 then implements `riscv_is_big_endian()` as `harts->harts[0].cfg.big_endian` and uses that single value for shared reset data, firmware info, and image loading. If a hart array contains mixed-endian CPUs, secondary harts will observe boot data encoded for hart 0 rather than for themselves.
findings.suggestion: Either reject mixed-endian hart arrays during machine initialization and document that boot-time endianness must be uniform, or rework boot/reset data generation so it is per-hart instead of derived from `harts[0]`.
findings.confidence_reason: The mismatch is visible from the new per-CPU property and the new helper implementation that always reads only hart 0.

disclaimer: LLM-generated draft. Not an authoritative review.
